stops.name: Vienna State Opera
stops.shortDescription: Opened in 1869 with a performance of Mozart's Don Giovanni, this Renaissance Revival building is one of the premier opera houses in the world. It was largely destroyed in WWII but meticulously reconstructed.

stops.name: Albertina
stops.shortDescription: Housed in the largest Habsburg residential palace, this museum holds one of the most important print rooms in the world. Its fully restored Habsburg State Rooms offer a glimpse into imperial living.

stops.name: Hofburg Palace
stops.shortDescription: The sprawling former principal imperial winter residence of the Habsburg dynasty. Today it serves as the official residence and workplace of the President of Austria.

stops.name: Heldenplatz
stops.shortDescription: Heroes' Square is a historic public plaza in front of the Hofburg Palace, framed by two monumental equestrian statues. It gained dark historical infamy in 1938 when Adolf Hitler announced the Austrian Anschluss from its balcony.

stops.name: Graben
stops.shortDescription: One of the most famous and exclusive streets in Vienna's innermost district. Its focal point is the elaborate Pestsäule (Plague Column), erected after the Great Plague epidemic of 1679.

stops.name: St. Stephen's Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: The mother church of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Vienna, featuring a stunning multi-colored tile roof. Its towering south spire, affectionately known as 'Steffl', has dominated the city skyline for centuries.

stops.name: Mozarthaus Vienna
stops.shortDescription: The only surviving Viennese residence of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. He lived in this elegant apartment from 1784 to 1787, famously composing 'The Marriage of Figaro' here.

stops.name: Kärntner Straße
stops.shortDescription: Vienna's most famous shopping street, completely pedestrianized. It runs straight from St. Stephen's Cathedral down to the State Opera, lined with historic cafes, street musicians, and boutiques.

stops.name: Imperial Crypt (Kaisergruft)
stops.shortDescription: Located beneath the Capuchin Church, this somber crypt is the principal place of entombment for members of the House of Habsburg, housing 149 elaborate metal sarcophagi.

stops.name: Burggarten
stops.shortDescription: A beautiful, tranquil park originally laid out as the private garden of the emperor. It features the elegant Palmenhaus glasshouse and the famous marble statue of Mozart.

stops.name: Kunsthistorisches Museum
stops.shortDescription: The Museum of Fine Arts, an architectural masterpiece built by Emperor Franz Joseph. It houses the vast, breathtaking art collections amassed by the Habsburgs over centuries.

stops.name: Maria-Theresien-Platz
stops.shortDescription: A grand public square flanked by two identical museum buildings. At its center stands a massive, intricate monument dedicated to Empress Maria Theresa, a key Habsburg monarch.

stops.name: Austrian Parliament Building
stops.shortDescription: A stunning example of Greek Revival architecture on the Ringstrasse, designed to symbolize the birthplace of democracy. It features the grand Pallas Athene fountain directly in front.

stops.name: Rathaus (City Hall)
stops.shortDescription: A massive Neo-Gothic building completed in 1883. Its spectacular plaza hosts Vienna's most famous and magical Christmas Market during the winter season and film festivals in the summer.

stops.name: Burgtheater
stops.shortDescription: The Austrian National Theatre, considered one of the most important German-language theaters in the world. It features a spectacular grand staircase with ceiling frescoes by Gustav Klimt.
